content_analysis.research_question: How to create an ultra-fast, all-in-one FASTQ preprocessor that integrates quality control, adapter trimming, filtering, and other functions into a single scan of the data to improve efficiency and speed over existing multi-tool workflows.
methods: Multi-threading support using a thread pool and data packing (N=1000 reads per pack)
Single-scan processing to minimize I/O overhead
K-mer based automatic adapter detection for single-end data
Overlap-analysis based adapter detection and base correction for paired-end data
PolyG/PolyX tail trimming for Illumina two-color chemistry data
UMI (Unique Molecular Identifier) preprocessing and shifting
Output splitting (by line number or file number) for parallel downstream pipelines

findings.finding: fastp is significantly faster than traditional tools, often 2–5 times faster than Trimmomatic or Cutadapt.
findings.evidence: In a speed evaluation on a 9316 M base dataset, fastp (PE) took 13.3 minutes compared to FASTQC (25.8 min), Cutadapt (24.6 min), and Trimmomatic (60.9 min).

findings.finding: fastp provides superior adapter trimming performance compared to sequence-matching tools.
findings.evidence: Table 2 shows fastp had the lowest mismatched bases (10.6M) and clipped reads (0.30M) compared to Raw data or other tools like SOAPnuke (3.46M clipped) and Cutadapt (1.05M clipped).

findings.finding: fastp is highly efficient for UMI preprocessing.
findings.evidence: fastp is ~2.7 times faster than 'umis' and ~6.1 times faster than 'UMI-tools', processing 4 Gb of data in 4.6 minutes compared to 12.43 and 28.38 minutes respectively.

findings.finding: Using fastp improves the specificity of downstream variant calling.
findings.evidence: Data filtered by fastp resulted in the lowest number of false positive variants (6708) compared to Trimmomatic (7174) and SOAPnuke (7040).

figures.description: Workflow diagram of fastp showing the main workflow for paired-end data and the internal modules of the paired-end preprocessor.
A demonstration of the algorithm used to extend an adapter seed in both forward and backward directions to identify full adapter sequences.
Base content ratio curves showing the abnormal G curve caused by polyG tails before processing and the corrected curve after fastp preprocessing.
Bar chart illustrating duplication estimation, showing read percentages and mean GC ratios across different duplication levels.
Visualization of overrepresented sequence analysis results as they appear in the fastp HTML report.
Comparison of adapter trimming performance across different tools based on the number of suspected adapters remaining after various mismatch tolerances.

tables.description: Speed comparison of fastp against other common FASTQ tools in both single-end (SE) and paired-end (PE) modes.
Evaluation of filtering effectiveness by comparing mapping results (mismatches, clips, and single-read maps) on the reference genome hg19.
Performance comparison specifically for UMI preprocessing tasks between fastp and specialized UMI tools.

limitations: Adapter detection assumes only one adapter exists in the data.
Adapter detection assumes sequences exist only in the read tails.
Duplication analysis may still overestimate levels in high-depth sequencing where different fragments share starting sequences.
connections: AfterQC (previous tool developed by the same authors)
UMI-tools and umis (specialized UMI tools compared against)
BWA-MEM and Samtools (used for downstream analysis evaluation)

draft_questions.question: In a speed evaluation on a 9,316 M base dataset, what were the specific processing times for fastp (PE) compared to Trimmomatic?
draft_questions.expected_answer: In the evaluation, fastp (PE) took 13.3 minutes to process the dataset. In contrast, Trimmomatic required 60.9 minutes, making fastp approximately 4.5 times faster in this specific comparison.

draft_questions.question: What design choices and implementation strategies allow fastp to achieve higher performance and lower I/O overhead than traditional FASTQ tools?
draft_questions.expected_answer: fastp achieves high performance by being implemented in C++ and utilizing multi-threading via a thread pool with data packing (N=1000 reads per pack). To minimize I/O overhead, it employs a single-scan processing architecture, which allows it to perform multiple QC and filtering tasks in one pass rather than reading and writing data multiple times.

draft_questions.question: How does fastp's methodology for adapter detection differ between single-end and paired-end sequencing data?
draft_questions.expected_answer: For single-end data, fastp utilizes a K-mer based automatic adapter detection method. For paired-end data, it uses an overlap-analysis based approach, which not only detects adapters but also enables base correction in the overlapping regions of the reads.
